choiceresponse.label: Which high-level conclusions are supported when comparing interpretability across images, tabular data, and text? (Select all that apply.)

checkboxgroup.choice: Interpretability methods must be adapted to the data modality.
A single explanation framework works equally well for all model types and inputs.
Many interpretability challenges (local vs global, causality vs correlation) recur across modalities.
Explanations are most useful when tied to a specific decision or question.

div.p: Interpretability tools must match the structure of the data, meaning that across images, tabular data, and text the form of explanation must change; Grad‑CAM uses spatial heatmaps for images, SHAP provides feature attributions for tabular inputs, and LIME highlights word‑level contributions for text. However, despite these surface differences, the core interpretability challenges remain the same: distinguishing local from global explanations, avoiding causal overinterpretation, and understanding that explanations describe model behavior rather than reality. Finally, the examples show that explanations are most informative when they answer a specific question, such as why a particular image was labeled “harbor” or why a specific message was flagged as spam, rather than when used as abstract summaries.
